summary:
Cardiac Arrest & High-Quality CPR

Adult Chain of Survival

The Chain of Survival describes the critical actions that must occur rapidly and sequentially to maximize the chance of survival from cardiac arrest. Each link depends on the strength of the others — a weak link reduces survival:

  1. Immediate recognition of cardiac arrest and activation of the EMS system — Bystander recognition that the victim is unresponsive and not breathing normally (agonal gasping is not normal breathing). Call 9-1-1 immediately.
  2. Early high-quality CPR — Bystander CPR doubles or triples survival from cardiac arrest. Push hard, push fast, allow full chest recoil, minimize interruptions.
  3. Rapid defibrillation — For shockable rhythms (V-fib, pulseless V-tach), defibrillation within 3–5 minutes of collapse produces the highest survival rates. Public-access AED programs are critical.
  4. Advanced resuscitation (ALS) — Advanced airway placement, IV/IO access, drug administration (epinephrine, amiodarone), and identification of reversible causes.
  5. Post-cardiac-arrest care — Targeted temperature management (therapeutic hypothermia), optimization of oxygenation and ventilation, hemodynamic support, and early cardiac catheterization for STEMI patients.
  6. Recovery — Survivors need comprehensive care including neurological assessment, cardiac rehabilitation, psychosocial support, and planning for secondary prevention.
Key Concept: Bystander CPR is the most important link in the chain for most communities. It takes EMS an average of 6–10 minutes to arrive. Without bystander CPR, the patient's brain goes 6–10 minutes without perfusion. With effective bystander CPR, that time is still critical — but survival is 2–3 times higher.

Compression Rate: 100–120/min

Compressions must be delivered at a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute. This is faster than previously recommended (prior guidelines said "at least 100"). Compressions faster than 120/min lead to:

  • Incomplete chest recoil (the chest does not fully expand between compressions)
  • Reduced coronary perfusion pressure
  • Increased rescuer fatigue
  • Decreased compression depth

Use metronome guidance — chest compressions to the beat of "Stayin' Alive" (Bee Gees) or "Another One Bites the Dust" (Queen) are at approximately the correct rate. Many modern defibrillators and monitor/defibrillators have built-in metronomes and rate feedback.

Compression Depth: 2–2.4 inches (5–6 cm)

Compressions must be at least 2 inches (5 cm) deep but should not exceed 2.4 inches (6 cm). Excessive depth can cause injuries (rib fractures, sternal fractures, liver lacerations, cardiac contusions).

  • Use the heel of one hand, with the other hand on top, fingers interlocked
  • Position hands on the lower half of the sternum (between the nipples at the center of the chest)
  • Keep arms straight, shoulders directly over the patient's chest
  • Use upper body weight, not just arm muscles, to drive compressions
  • Avoid leaning on the chest between compressions

Full Chest Recoil

Complete chest recoil between each compression allows the heart to refill with blood. Incomplete recoil (leaning) reduces venous return to the right heart, decreases cardiac output, and increases intrathoracic pressure. The chest wall should fully expand to its neutral position after each compression. This is often the most neglected element of high-quality CPR.

Minimize Interruptions (Chest Compression Fraction >80%)

Chest Compression Fraction (CCF) is the percentage of total resuscitation time spent performing compressions. The target is >80%. Every pause in compressions — even for ventilation, defibrillation, or pulse checks — reduces coronary and cerebral perfusion pressure. Strategies to minimize interruptions:

  • Coordinate compressor changes every 2 minutes (or sooner if fatigued)
  • Defibrillate with minimal pause — ideally <5 seconds before and after shock
  • Limit pulse checks to ≤10 seconds — performed during rhythm analysis if using a monitor
  • Prepare for the next intervention (IV, airway) during ongoing compressions
  • Assign a team leader to monitor CCF and give real-time feedback

Compression-to-Ventilation Ratio: 30:2

For single-rescuer and two-rescuer CPR in adults, the ratio is 30 compressions to 2 ventilations. This ratio prioritizes continuous blood flow while providing adequate ventilation:

  • 30 compressions should take approximately 15–18 seconds (at 100–120/min)
  • Each ventilation should be delivered over 1 second, producing visible chest rise
  • Avoid excessive ventilation — it increases intrathoracic pressure (decreasing venous return) and causes gastric insufflation
  • If an advanced airway (ET tube, supraglottic airway) is in place, ventilations are given at 1 breath every 6 seconds (~10/min) with continuous compressions (no pause)
Clinical Pearl: Do NOT stop CPR to establish IV access or place an advanced airway. These procedures should be performed with ongoing chest compressions. The only reasons to pause chest compressions are: (1) rhythm analysis/shock delivery, (2) pulse check (≤10 s), (3) moving the patient, or (4) compressor switch (ideally <5 s pause).

2-Minute Cycles and Pulse Checks

CPR is organized into 2-minute cycles of continuous compressions (with 2 ventilations inserted after every 30 compressions). After each 2-minute cycle:

  • Pause briefly for rhythm analysis by the monitor/defibrillator
  • Check for a pulse (carotid or femoral) — this should take ≤10 seconds
  • If a shockable rhythm is present: defibrillate, then immediately resume CPR for another 2 minutes
  • If a non-shockable rhythm (PEA, asystole): resume CPR immediately — no shock
  • If a pulse is present (ROSC): begin post-cardiac-arrest care
  • Switch the compressor at each 2-minute mark to prevent fatigue-related quality decline

Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC)

ROSC is the resumption of a perfusing heart rhythm with a palpable pulse. Signs of ROSC include:

  • Sudden or sustained rise in EtCO₂ (typically >40 mmHg) — the most sensitive early indicator
  • Visible arterial waveform on invasive monitoring (if available)
  • Palpable carotid or femoral pulse
  • Sudden increase in BP (if monitored)
  • Spontaneous movements, breathing, or groaning

When ROSC is achieved: start post-cardiac-arrest care (optimize oxygenation to SpO₂ 94–98%, maintain BP, consider therapeutic hypothermia, transport to PCI-capable center). Do not re-check a pulse that was already positive — focus on stabilization.

Compression Quality Monitoring (EtCO₂)

End-tidal carbon dioxide (EtCO₂) monitoring via waveform capnography is the most valuable real-time feedback tool during CPR:

  • EtCO₂ >10–20 mmHg: Compressions are generating adequate cardiac output and pulmonary blood flow
  • EtCO₂ <10 mmHg: Compressions may be inadequate — adjust depth, rate, recoil; consider switch in compressor or addressing reversible causes
  • Sudden rise in EtCO₂ (to >40 mmHg): Highly suggestive of ROSC — stop compressions and check pulse
  • Sudden drop in EtCO₂: May indicate tube displacement (if advanced airway), pulmonary embolism, or loss of cardiac output
  • If available, arterial line diastolic pressure >25 mmHg correlates with adequate coronary perfusion pressure

Other feedback devices include: accelerometer-based compression sensors (provide real-time depth and rate feedback), defibrillator impedance waveform analysis, and CPR feedback pads (e.g., ZOLL R Series, Philips HeartStart MRx).

quiz.q: During a cardiac arrest, the monitor shows no change in EtCO₂ (stays at 8 mmHg) despite 6 minutes of CPR. The compressor appears fatigued and depth is decreasing. What should the team do?
options: Continue with the same compressor since changing hands interrupts compressions
Switch the compressor, verify compression depth and rate, and reassess EtCO₂
Increase the compression rate to 140/min to improve cardiac output
Administer a second dose of epinephrine before switching compressors

quiz.q: A patient in cardiac arrest has been intubated. What ventilation rate should be used with continuous chest compressions?
options: 1 breath every 3 seconds (20/min) to maximize oxygenation
1 breath every 6 seconds (~10/min) to avoid excessive ventilation
2 breaths every 30 compressions, pausing for each breath
No ventilation — only compressions until ROSC is achieved

quiz.q: During a 2-minute rhythm check, the monitor shows organized rhythm and the EtCO₂ suddenly rises from 12 mmHg to 52 mmHg. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The patient is being hyperventilated by the BVM
The patient has achieved ROSC — check for a pulse
The EtCO₂ monitor is malfunctioning — replace the sensor
Epinephrine administration has increased metabolic CO₂ production

flashcards.question: What are the six links in the Adult Chain of Survival?
flashcards.answer: (1) Recognition of arrest + activation of EMS, (2) Early high-quality CPR, (3) Rapid defibrillation, (4) Advanced resuscitation (ALS), (5) Post-cardiac-arrest care, (6) Recovery.

flashcards.question: What is the recommended compression rate and compression depth for adult CPR?
flashcards.answer: Rate: 100–120 compressions per minute. Depth: 2–2.4 inches (5–6 cm). Compressions faster than 120/min reduce recoil, depth, and coronary perfusion.

flashcards.question: What is Chest Compression Fraction (CCF) and what is the target?
flashcards.answer: CCF is the percentage of total resuscitation time spent performing compressions. The target is >80% — meaning compressions are being performed for more than 80% of the arrest time.

flashcards.question: What is the adult compression-to-ventilation ratio for single-rescuer and two-rescuer CPR?
flashcards.answer: 30 compressions to 2 ventilations (30:2). If an advanced airway is in place, ventilate at 1 breath every 6 seconds (~10/min) with continuous compressions.

flashcards.question: What is the duration of a CPR cycle and when should the compressor be switched?
flashcards.answer: Each cycle is 2 minutes. The compressor should be switched every 2 minutes (or sooner if fatigued) to maintain compression quality.

flashcards.question: What does an EtCO₂ value below 10 mmHg during CPR indicate?
flashcards.answer: Inadequate cardiac output — compressions may be ineffective. Reassess compression depth, rate, and recoil. Consider switch in compressor or identifying reversible causes of arrest.

flashcards.question: What is the most sensitive early indicator of ROSC?
flashcards.answer: A sudden, sustained rise in EtCO₂ to >40 mmHg during CPR. This suggests the heart has resumed pumping blood and CO₂ is being delivered to the lungs for exhalation. Check for a pulse.

flashcards.question: Why is full chest recoil important during CPR?
flashcards.answer: Full chest recoil allows the heart to refill with blood during the relaxation phase. Incomplete recoil (leaning) reduces venous return, decreases cardiac output, and increases intrathoracic pressure.

critical.scenario: You and your partner respond to a witnessed cardiac arrest at a gym. A bystander started CPR immediately — approximately 2 minutes ago according to the time on the AED. The patient is a 60-year-old male with no known medical history who collapsed while running on a treadmill. The AED was placed and gave one shock before your arrival. You take over the scene. The patient is now in the 'CPR' phase of the AED cycle. Your partner begins high-quality compressions while you prepare airway equipment and apply your monitor. The EtCO₂ reading on your monitor shows 14 mmHg after 1 minute of your team's compressions.
critical.question: What actions should you prioritize at this point, and how do you interpret the EtCO₂ value?

options.t: Stop CPR to place an advanced airway, then resume compressions. The EtCO₂ of 14 mmHg indicates adequate perfusion — no changes needed.
options.f: Incorrect. CPR should not be stopped for airway placement. An EtCO₂ of 14 mmHg is borderline — acceptable (>10) but not optimal. Focus on maximizing compression quality.

options.t: Continue high-quality CPR with 30:2 ratio, minimize interruptions, and analyze rhythm on the monitor when the AED cycle completes. The EtCO₂ of 14 mmHg suggests adequate but not optimal compressions — coach the compressor on depth and rate.
options.f: Correct. An EtCO₂ >10 mmHg indicates compressions are generating some cardiac output. The goal is to optimize quality (depth 2-2.4", rate 100-120, full recoil, CCF >80%) while preparing for rhythm analysis and defibrillation if indicated. Place the advanced airway during ongoing compressions, not during a pause.

critical.explanation: An EtCO₂ >10 mmHg during CPR suggests compressions are generating measurable pulmonary blood flow. Values >20 mmHg are associated with improved outcomes. The team should optimize compression quality, ventilate at 1 breath q6s (if advanced airway) or 30:2 (if BVM), and prepare for rhythm analysis. If EtCO₂ falls below 10 mmHg, the compressor should be switched and depth/rate/recoil reassessed.

critical.scenario: You are performing two-rescuer CPR on a 72-year-old female in cardiac arrest. The cardiac monitor shows fine V-fib. The EtCO₂ reading is 8 mmHg after 4 minutes of CPR. Your partner is the compressor and is visibly tiring — compressions are becoming visibly shallower (about 1.5 inches deep) and the rate is dropping to around 90/min. You notice your partner is leaning on the patient's chest between compressions. The nearest hospital is 15 minutes away. ALS is 8 minutes out.
critical.question: What is the most critical immediate action to improve the quality of resuscitation?

options.t: Continue with the same compressor — changing hands wastes time. Increase the ventilation rate to compensate for poor compressions.
options.f: Incorrect. Fatigued compressors produce inadequate depth, improper rate, and leaning — all of which dramatically reduce survival. Continuing with a fatigued compressor is harmful.

options.t: Switch compressors immediately — have the new compressor focus on achieving 2" depth, 100–120/min rate, and full chest recoil. Coach the previous compressor on proper technique. Prepare to defibrillate at the next rhythm check.
options.f: Correct. Fatigued compressors cannot maintain high-quality CPR. Studies show compression quality degrades within 1–2 minutes. Rotating every 2 minutes (or sooner if visibly fatigued) is essential. The EtCO₂ of 8 mmHg suggests inadequate cardiac output — improving compression quality should increase it. Fine V-fib may become coarse V-fib with better compressions, increasing defibrillation success.

critical.explanation: Compressor fatigue is one of the most common causes of poor CPR quality. The EtCO₂ of 8 mmHg (<10) confirms that compressions are inadequate. The team should switch compressors, correct the depth (2 inches minimum) and recoil (no leaning), and prepare for defibrillation at the next analysis. If ALS arrives in 8 minutes, they can establish IV/IO access and administer epinephrine and amiodarone.

critical.scenario: You are treating a 55-year-old male in cardiac arrest. The arrest was witnessed, bystander CPR was started within 2 minutes, and you have been on scene for 10 minutes. The patient has received one shock for V-fib and 2 doses of epinephrine. Your partner is performing compressions at a rate of 115/min with good depth. During the next rhythm check, you see organized P waves at 80/min with narrow QRS complexes. The EtCO₂, which was 11 mmHg, has rapidly risen to 48 mmHg. Your partner stops compressions and you feel a strong carotid pulse.
critical.question: What phase of care are you now entering and what are your immediate priorities?

options.t: This is ROSC. Your immediate priorities are: check for a pulse (if confirmed, stop compressions), optimize oxygenation (SpO₂ target 94–98%), maintain BP (IV fluids/vasopressors if hypotensive), obtain a 12-lead ECG, and transport to a PCI-capable center. Consider therapeutic hypothermia.
options.f: Correct. ROSC has been achieved (organized rhythm + EtCO₂ rise + palpable pulse). Compressions stop. The focus shifts to post-cardiac-arrest care: airway management, hemodynamic support, reperfusion (cath lab activation), and temperature management. Do not re-check the pulse — you have confirmed it.

options.t: This is PEA with an organized rhythm — the rising EtCO₂ is from the epinephrine. Continue CPR and administer a third dose of epinephrine. Do not stop compressions.
options.f: Incorrect. A sudden rise in EtCO₂ to >40 mmHg with an organized rhythm and palpable pulse is ROSC — not PEA. Continuing CPR after ROSC can cause injury (rib fractures, organ damage). The epinephrine does not cause a sudden EtCO₂ rise.

critical.explanation: ROSC is defined by the presence of a palpable pulse with organized cardiac activity. The sudden EtCO₂ rise (from 11 to 48 mmHg) is the earliest objective indicator — it signals that cardiac output has resumed and CO₂ is being transported to the lungs. Post-ROSC care priorities: oxygenate to SpO₂ 94–98% (avoid hyperoxia), maintain systolic BP >90–100 mmHg (fluids, norepinephrine if available), obtain 12-lead ECG to identify STEMI, transport to PCI-capable center, and initiate targeted temperature management (32–36°C) per protocol.
critical.kpi: ROSC recognition and post-arrest care transition
